Characters without the skill move at half their normal rate. Increasing the rate adds +10 difficulty for each additional one-half of the base climbing Move.

A character's total leaping distance from a standing position equals one-quarter of Move in meters, rounded up. Base difficulty is Very Easy to move this distance, +10 for each additional two meters. A running start can add +5 to the skill total per round, up to +10, if the running difficulty was beaten in both rounds.

rows: Fewer than basic handholds and footholds.
blocks.title: Climbing Modifiers

rows: Performing the maneuver from a flat surface to another flat surface.
+3 or more to the jumper's difficulty
Rough, slick or unsteady landing area.
blocks.title: Jumping Modifiers

blocks.text: Lifting is a character's ability to lift heavy objects and carry something for a long time. The difficulty depends on the weight of the object and how long it will be carried.

When a character first lifts an object, make a lifting check. At each interval on the chart, the character must make another check to continue carrying the object. Failure means the object must be put down immediately.

If the character matches the difficulty level, he can do nothing but concentrate on carrying the object while walking. If he beats the difficulty by more than one level, he can do other actions, but lifting always counts as an action.

After the first hour, the character must make a new lifting or Strength roll every hour at the same difficulty as for one hour. On failure, the character must rest for twice as long as he was lifting the weight.

fields.time_taken: Stamina checks are made when characters exert themselves over long periods of time

blocks.text: Stamina checks reflect that a character is being pushed to physical limits. They should be called for once in a while to show strain, and only when a character does something out of the ordinary.
Whenever a character fails a stamina roll, he is fatigued; all actions are at -1D for every failed stamina check until the character rests for as long as he exerted himself.

Characters can continue if fatigued until they fail a third stamina check. At that point, the character is completely exhausted and must rest for twice the amount of time exerted to remove the penalty.

Platt Okeefe marches through scorching badlands. The gamemaster calls for one stamina roll per hour at Difficult. After failing checks, Platt suffers cumulative -1D penalties until the third failure leaves her exhausted.

fields.time_taken: One round to several minutes or hours

blocks.text: This skill represents the character's ability to stay afloat in aquatic environments: lakes, oceans, flooding rivers and swimming pools. Swimming difficulties are determined by water conditions.

When a character fails a swimming check, he begins to drown. Roll 2D at the beginning of each round; if the total is less than the number of rounds the character has been drowning, the character drowns and dies.

Characters can attempt other actions while drowning at a -3D penalty. They may attempt to save themselves once per round with a swimming total at one difficulty level higher than the failed swimming roll; this roll does not suffer the -3D penalty.

Technical stands for "technical aptitude" and represents a character's innate knowledge of how to take apart, repair and modify things. Technical also reflects knowledge of healing and medicine, setting explosives, and figuring out electronic security systems. Characters with a low Technical have trouble changing a power pack in a blaster.

The various repair skills follow similar patterns for time taken and difficulties. Several repair skill rolls can be made when fixing damaged vehicles and equipment. The initial roll represents 15 minutes of work; if it fails, additional rolls may be made after varying lengths of time.

Each damaged system aboard a vehicle or vessel requires a separate repair roll. No single repair roll fixes all systems if multiple components are damaged, but several characters may divide the work and repair different systems at the same time.

The difficulty level for repair rolls depends on how much damage the item, vehicle or starship has taken. Repair times, difficulties and costs are generalizations and can be adjusted to suit the game.

rows: Previously built or modified the item; intimately familiar with it.
Common item, all parts available, character has a general tool kit.
Has seen but not used item.
Has never seen item; only some parts available.

blocks.text: When a drive or generator is destroyed, a Difficult repair roll is necessary to replace it, and it costs 35% of the craft's original value. Use the appropriate vehicle or vessel repair skill.
A Moderate repair roll fixes a damaged hyperdrive. Use capital ship repair, space transports repair or starfighter repair for the appropriate starship type.

Characters can use their repair skills to improve the speed, damage, shields or other stats of vehicles, vessels and weapons. Improvements are balanced by cost, technician talent, strain on existing systems and the risk of quirks or failures.

Stats may only be increased one pip, one Move level or one hyperdrive level at a time. No system may be improved more than +1D+2, or more than 4 moves, using these rules. A new repair improvement roll can be made every month of game time.
If characters hire someone else to implement modifications, double or triple the cost. Failed modification rolls might permanently damage or destroy the system being modified.

Whenever a player rolls a 1 on the Wild Die while using a modified device, the gamemaster may roll 1D for a mishap and add the system's mishap modifier. Use the appropriate Weapon, Vehicle or Non-Lethal Mishap table.

rows: The weapon stops working, but need only be slammed hard against something to work again, or recalibrated for a starship or heavy weapon; this takes an action.
The weapon is broken and cannot be repaired.
The weapon explodes, doing damage equal to the amount of the modification bonus.
blocks.title: Weapon Mishap

rows: The failing system or the vehicle suffers a temporary power loss and maintains speed and heading for this round. Kicking or hitting a random control panel fixes it; this takes an action.
The vehicle bucks, or the affected system sparks and smokes. An Easy operation roll keeps control, and an Easy repair roll fixes the smoking system.
All power systems shut down and the vehicle drops like a stone. A Moderate operation roll is required for a safe emergency landing; otherwise the vehicle crashes.
blocks.title: Vehicle Mishap

rows: The item stops working, but works again if slammed hard against something; this takes an action.
The item is broken and cannot be repaired.
blocks.title: Non-Lethal Mishap
Shield or Weapon Die Code Increase

blocks.text: The rules for modifying weapons also apply for improving personal armor and general equipment. Use the charts above to determine the difficulty and cost to boost armor and equipment die codes.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: Oceanic transport, coastal runabout, skiff, sailboat

blocks.text: This skill represents a character's familiarity with the workings of aquatic vehicles, and his ability to repair and modify them. Each roll may repair one damaged system aboard a particular vessel. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is damaged and what systems have been hit.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then two hours. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: Armor repair reflects the character's ability to fix and modify armor that has been damaged. The cost and difficulty to repair armor depends upon how badly damaged it is. See "Weapons" in "Using Repair Skills" above.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then two hours. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: blaster pistols, surface-to-surface blaster artillery, heavy blaster cannon

blocks.text: A character's ability to fix and modify blaster weapons (character-, speeder- and walker-scale) is represented by blaster repair. The cost and difficulty depend upon how badly the weapon is damaged.
Vibroblades, slugthrowers and other non-blaster weapons are repaired and modified using the equipment repair skill.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.specializations: Type or class of capital ship
fields.examples: Imperial Star Destroyer, Victory Star Destroyer, Nebulon-B frigate

blocks.text: This skill represents a character's familiarity with the workings of capital ships and his ability to repair them. Each roll may repair one damaged component aboard a vessel. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is damaged and what systems have been damaged.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: concussion missiles, gravity well projectors, ion cannons, laser cannon, proton torpedoes, tractor beams, turbolaser

blocks.text: Capital ship weapon repair is used to repair capital-scale ship weapons. The cost and difficulty depend upon how badly the weapon is damaged.

fields.time_taken: One round to several minutes, hours or days
fields.examples: portable computer, mainframe, datapad, YT-1300 computer

blocks.text: Characters use this skill to repair, modify and program computers. It also covers familiarity with computer security procedures, the ability to evade them and the ability to get secure information.

Getting information through a computer requires connecting to the appropriate network and locating the files sought. Connecting to a network takes about one round and requires no roll if the computer is already linked. Establishing a remote connection requires an Easy computer programming/repair roll.
Finding information on a single topic requires time, then a computer programming/repair roll. The time required and difficulty depend on the type of information sought.

rows: Public data, such as the character's credit balance. (1 minute)
Public data requiring a little more research, such as who owns a building or starship. (10 minutes)
Private data, such as a person's diary or credit balance. (1 hour)
Secret data, such as a corporation's records or military plans. (8 hours)
Top-secret data, such as cloaking device plans or a Grand Moff's itinerary, at gamemaster discretion.
Information only a select few individuals would know, at gamemaster discretion.

blocks.text: If the character beats the difficulty number, he retrieves the information. If the roll is less than half the difficulty number, the computer's security system becomes aware of the intruder and triggers alarms.
Demolitions reflects a character's ability to set explosives for destructive purposes and to accomplish specific special effects.

The most common explosive is detonite; a fist-sized cube does 1D speeder-scale damage. Additional cubes increase base damage by +1D. Alternative explosives made from available materials are usually less effective.
When setting charges, a character can try to cause extra damage or create special effects.

rows: A lightly armored door such as a blast door.
A heavily armored object, such as the hull of a starship.
blocks.title: Extra Damage Difficulty

rows: Rigging a bomb so the next time a speeder is put into drive the bomb goes off, destroying the vehicle.
Blowing off the back hatch of a computer without leaving permanent marks or burns.
Rigging and hiding explosives on blast doors so the next time they close the blast funnels down a specific hall.
Setting a charge on an AT-AT leg so the drive cords are severed and the walker topples when it tries to step.
blocks.title: Special Effects Difficulty

fields.time_taken: 15 minutes to several hours or days
fields.examples: astromech droid, protocol droid, probe droid

blocks.text: Characters may use this skill to program a droid to learn a new skill or task. While droids can learn by trial and error, it is often easier and faster to program the activity into the droid's memory.
The programmer must have access to a computer or datapad jacked directly into the droid's memory.

The difficulty depends on the task's sophistication and can be modified by other factors. Time also depends on complexity: a Very Easy task might take half an hour, while a Very Difficult task might take days or weeks.

Droid owners often wipe intelligent droids' memories. A memory wipe eliminates all learned skills and tasks not hard-wired into permanent memory. If a skill is directly hardwired, increase the programming difficulty by +30.
When a new skill is programmed into a droid, it begins at one pip over the attribute. Once learned, the droid may spend Character Points or use skill cartridges to improve it.

rows: Move boxes from one area to another, one at a time, making neat stacks.
Patrol an area in a pattern and report or stop unauthorized intruders; performing a memory wipe.
Handle a hotel front desk, be courteous and solve customer service problems.
Teach a droid a rudimentary skill use.
Modify vehicles or equipment for better performance by making assumptions rather than relying on established fact.
Design a factory-made droid in a manner that overrides core programming, such as changing a protocol droid into an effective combat droid or reprogramming personality.
Droid type is completely unfamiliar to programmer.
Task is not related to the kinds of tasks the droid is designed to undertake.
Droid is of very low intelligence and incapable of creative thought.
Task is distantly related to the droid's designated functions.
Task is close to, but not exactly, the type of task or skill the droid is designed for.
Droid is of low intelligence and is not intended for creative thought.
Droid model is familiar to programmer.
+5 or more to the character's roll
Droid is fully sapient and intended to make independent conclusions and decisions.
+5 or more to the character's roll
Droid has been operating independently for a long time.

fields.time_taken: 15 minutes, then one hour, then two hours; may be several hours or days depending upon the level of repair
fields.examples: astromech droid, protocol droid, probe droid

blocks.text: This skill represents a character's talent to repair, modify and maintain droids. The repair difficulty depends on how badly damaged the droid is; the cost reflects replacement parts based on the droid's original value.
Droid repair tasks can be broken down into smaller, easier, more time-consuming tasks, so unless a droid was destroyed, it can often be repaired with enough time, patience and money.
Modifications depend on parts availability, attachment complexity and droid configuration. Attachments often include software that must be programmed or hardwired into memory using droid programming.

fields.advanced: requires prerequisite skills; each specialization has its own prerequisites

blocks.text: Like other advanced skills, (A) engineering has a higher Character Point cost and requires prerequisite skills. Each specialization has different prerequisites.

The (A) engineering skill involves many separate and complex activities and fields of study. An engineer is part architect, technician, repairman, inventor and scientist, and must be familiar with every aspect of the specialization.
Using (A) engineering specializations allows a character to build, maintain, repair or modify within that field.

The (A) engineering skill is generally left to gamemaster discretion. Gamemasters should be cautious about allowing very powerful or unbalanced designs, and may require multiple rolls at varying difficulties, significant time and work crews for prototypes.

At gamemaster discretion, if the appropriate (A) engineering skill is used to modify a component instead of the repair skill, the system is no longer considered jury-rigged. Mishap modifiers may be ignored if a mishap roll is required, and the modification may be attempted at one Difficulty Level lower than indicated.

rows: Design aquatic vehicles and aquatic vehicle systems.
5D capital ship repair, 4D capital ship weapon repair, 2D computer programming/repair
Design capital ships and capital ship systems; missing prerequisites restrict bonus effects.
1D bureaucracy, 2D business, 3D demolitions, 1D law enforcement, 2D in any two repair skills
Build, maintain and modify settlements, civic projects, businesses and factories.
Design and construct computers, software and complex networks.
Design and construct small equipment items and non-blaster weapons.
Design ground vehicles and vehicle systems.
Design hover vehicles and hover vehicle systems.
2D computer programming/repair, 2D demolitions, and 1D in two relevant engineering skills
Construct fortified bases, strongholds and military installations.
5D starfighter repair and 3D starship weapons repair
Design starfighters and systems; missing weapon prerequisite restricts bonus effects to non-weapon functions.
5D in any two of blaster repair, capital ship weapon repair, demolitions, equipment repair, starship weapon repair
Design and construct weapons systems and blasters.
blocks.title: Engineering Prerequisites

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then two hours. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: This skill covers fixing and modifying character-, speeder- and walker-scale equipment items, including non-blaster and non-energy beam weapons. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly the equipment is damaged.
Blasters and other energy weapons use blaster repair. Computers and small sensors use computer programming/repair.
First aid reflects a character's ability to perform emergency life-saving procedures in the field. See "Combat and Injuries" for rules on using first aid.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: This skill represents a character's familiarity with the workings of ground vehicles and his ability to modify or repair them. Each roll may repair one damaged system aboard a particular craft.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: Characters use this skill when repairing or modifying hover vehicle systems. Each roll may repair one damaged component aboard a particular craft.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. Construction takes approximately one month

blocks.text: Characters use this skill when repairing, modifying or building a lightsaber. It is generally restricted to Jedi Knights who learned it from their master. Lightsaber repair/engineering combines repair and design due to the special nature of the skill.

To construct a lightsaber from basic components takes a minimum of one month and requires a successful lightsaber repair roll against a Very Difficult difficulty. Decrease the difficulty by one level for each extra month spent building the saber, to a minimum of Easy.

Jedi characters may adjust a lightsaber's base damage die code through modifications. This skill is not advanced, but should be judiciously governed by the gamemaster and generally used only by Jedi characters.

fields.advanced: requires first aid of at least 5D

blocks.text: Characters with this skill can perform complex medical procedures such as surgery, operation of bacta tanks and installation of cybernetic replacements and enhancements. They are familiar with all types of medicines and can use them to best effect.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: XP-38 landspeeder, Incom T-16 Skyhopper, Rebel Alliance combat snowspeeder

blocks.text: Repulsorlift repair represents a character's affinity for repairing and modifying vehicles with repulsorlift generators. Each roll may repair one damaged system aboard a particular vehicle.

fields.examples: magna lock, blast door, retinal lock

blocks.text: This skill represents a character's knowledge of physical security systems: locks, alarm systems and other detection devices. It does not govern computer security procedures.

rows: Super-security lock, such as an Imperial governor, Fleet captain, rare collectible dealer or Jabba the Hutt would use.
If Darth Vader or Emperor Palpatine locked things up, this is what they would use.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized by circumstances
fields.examples: YT-1300 transport, Gallofree medium transport, Corellian Action VI transport

blocks.text: Characters with this skill can repair and modify space transports. Each roll may repair one damaged component aboard a particular transport. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is damaged and what systems have been hit.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: X-wing, TIE/In, TIE interceptor, Z-95 Headhunter

blocks.text: This repair skill represents a character's ability to fix and modify starfighters. Each roll may repair one damaged system aboard a particular fighter. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is damaged and what systems have been hit.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances
fields.examples: concussion missiles, ion cannons, laser cannon, proton torpedoes, turbolasers

blocks.text: Starship weapon repair covers a character's ability to fix and modify starfighter-scale weapons. The cost and difficulty depend upon how badly the weapon is damaged.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then one hour, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: This repair skill represents a character's ability to fix and modify submersible vehicles. Each roll may repair one damaged system aboard a particular submersible. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is damaged and what systems have been hit.

fields.time_taken: In general, 15 minutes, then six hours, then one day, then two days. May be customized to circumstances

blocks.text: Characters use this skill when repairing or modifying walker systems. Each roll may fix one damaged component aboard a particular walker. The cost and difficulty depend on how badly it is hit and what systems have been damaged.

There are plenty of other skills in the galaxy, but most of them do not directly affect the game, so they are not covered here. When a character wants to do something not covered by a skill, the gamemaster can assign the action to an attribute; if the player wants the character to improve, the gamemaster can invent a new skill.

For example, a sport like nega-ball has no listed skill. The gamemaster might use Dexterity because it involves running around and throwing a ball at a small target, or might create a nega-ball skill if the player wants to improve.

The Star Wars game system is flexible. The gamemaster could instead use thrown weapons or grenade for throwing, running for movement and stamina for endurance. The right choice depends on what the gamemaster and players feel is fair.

Gamemasters may reject any new skill or specialization they think is unbalanced, or give the character complications for possessing it. A character with communications: Imperial secret codes, for example, may draw dangerous Imperial attention.
